segments.title: Soviet Cold War destroyers as visible fleet presence
segments.summary: The lecture argues that Soviet Cold War destroyers carried much of the visible surface presence usually overshadowed by submarines, cruisers, and carriers.
segments.body: The discussion captures the episode's organizing argument. Dr. Clarke opens by saying destroyers are often left out of Cold War naval memory even though they were central surface combatants, then follows Gorshkov's use of destroyer construction, missile upgrades, and visible surface ships to build practical Soviet presence. The Cuban Missile Crisis section reinforces the point by contrasting submarines' combat power with the surface fleet's diplomatic and escort visibility.

evidence.note: The introduction contrasts common Cold War naval memory with the claim that destroyers were among the most important surface vessels.
Gorshkov's early fight for destroyers is tied to missile adoption, numbers, and capability growth for the Soviet fleet.
The passage frames surface ships as adaptable, visible instruments of presence and says Gorshkov carried out practical naval power through the destroyer force.
The Cuban Missile Crisis section argues that submarines could not provide visible escort or demonstration effects, pushing Soviet attention back toward destroyers and surface power.
